content.plain_english: This week in Year 4 maths, your child is working on Time. The learning focuses on: read, write and convert time between analogue and digital 12-hour and 24-hour clocks; solve problems involving converting from:; and hours to minutes. These skills build directly on what they have already mastered and prepare them for the next steps in the programme of study.
content.in_school: Children use practical measuring equipment — rulers, scales, measuring cylinders, and clocks — to explore measurement in real contexts. They record findings using diagrams and number lines before progressing to written calculations. Problem-solving tasks show how these skills are used every day.
content.home_activity: Talk through the family routine using time language: 'We leave at quarter to 9', 'lunch is at half past 12'. Ask your child to read the time on an analogue clock whenever they pass one. Time simple activities — how long does it take to brush your teeth?
dinner_table_questions: Can you show me how to read, write and convert time between analogue and digital 12-hour and 24-hour clocks?
Was it easy or tricky to solve problems involving converting from:? Why?
How would you explain today's maths to someone who has never seen it before?

key_vocabulary.definition: A small unit of time — 60 seconds = 1 minute.
A unit of time — 60 minutes = 1 hour.
A unit of time — 24 hours = 1 day.
A clock with hands that show the time on a circular face.
How long something takes from start to finish.

example_questions: A train leaves at 11:48 and arrives at 13:07. How long is the journey?
Convert 3.5 hours to hours and minutes.
What time is 2 h 45 min before midnight?
content.times_table_tip: Year 4 children should know all their times tables to 12 × 12 by the end of the year — the national Multiplication Tables Check happens in Year 4. Daily 3-minute practice mixing tables in random order is the best preparation.
